title: The part that uses food to manage emotions it cannot name

htmlContent:
The one who reaches for food when feelings get too loud

I know this part. I've sat with her at 2am staring into the refrigerator light. She's the one who tells me I'm not hungry but my hand is already opening the pantry door. She's the one who promises that one more bite will quiet whatever is churning in my chest. And she's the one who hates herself the next morning, staring at the empty wrapper, swearing it won't happen again.

But it does. Because she's not the problem. She's the solution.

Here's the thing. Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades studying how trauma gets stored in the body. He found that when we can't name what we feel, we find other ways to manage it. Food is one of the most available tools. It's legal. It's socially acceptable. And it works, at least in the moment.

That part of you that reaches for food when emotions feel too big? She's not weak. She's not broken. She's not failing at self-control. She's a protector. She's doing exactly what she learned to do, probably before you had words for what was happening to you.

Why words matter more than willpower

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about something called "pendulation." It's the natural rhythm of the nervous system, moving between activation and settling. When we experience trauma, that rhythm gets stuck. We get locked in hyperarousal (anxiety, panic, rage) or hypoarousal (numbness, collapse, dissociation). Either way, the body is stuck.

Food changes that. It's a direct intervention on the nervous system. Chewing activates the vagus nerve. Swallowing triggers a primal safety response. Sugar floods the brain with dopamine, the same chemical that lights up when we connect with someone we love. Carbohydrates increase serotonin, the neurotransmitter that helps us feel calm and stable.

So when that part of you reaches for food, she's not trying to ruin your diet. She's trying to regulate your nervous system. She's trying to get you out of freeze. She's trying to lower the volume on a panic attack you don't even recognize as a panic attack because you've been living in that state for so long it feels normal.

Think about it. When was the last time you ate when you were genuinely hungry, stopped when you were full, and didn't think about food again until your body actually needed fuel? For most of us with complex trauma, that experience is foreign. We don't eat based on interoceptive cues. We eat based on emotional states we cannot name.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who trained under Bessel van der Kolk, talks about this directly. She says that parts of us often carry emotions that the conscious mind cannot tolerate. The part that uses food is often holding feelings that were never allowed to exist in your family. Rage that got punished. Grief that was dismissed. Loneliness that was ignored. That part learned early that the only acceptable way to feel anything was through something you could put in your mouth.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker wrote it specifically for people who grew up in unpredictable or abusive environments. He names the inner critic, the abandonment depression, the emotional flashbacks that drive so much of our compulsive behavior. It helps to have a map.

The part that learned food was safe when people weren't

Let me tell you a story. When I was a kid, my parents fought constantly. Not the loud kind, the cold kind. The silence that filled the house like poison gas. I didn't have words for what I felt. I just knew that my chest hurt and my stomach was tight and I wanted to disappear.

But the pantry was safe. The pantry didn't scream at me. The pantry didn't ignore me. The pantry didn't leave. And when I ate, something shifted. The tightness eased. The world got softer. I could breathe again.

That's not a character flaw. That's a survival strategy. And it worked. It kept me alive through years of emotional neglect and confusion. It gave me something to hold onto when there was nothing else.

But here's what I didn't understand then. That part of me wasn't just seeking food. She was seeking connection. She was seeking comfort. She was seeking the regulation that should have come from a caregiver's attuned presence. She was using food as a substitute for what was missing, because food was available and people were not.

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems (IFS), calls this a "firefighter." Firefighter parts are the ones that jump in when a younger, wounded part gets triggered. They don't care about long-term consequences. They care about putting out the fire right now. And food is one of the most effective fire extinguishers we have. It's immediate. It's reliable. It never says no.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz wrote it to help people understand that every part, even the ones we hate, is trying to help. The part that eats when it's not hungry is not your enemy. She's your most loyal protector. She just needs a different job.

What happens when you stop fighting the part

I spent years trying to control my eating. Diets. Meal plans. Calorie counting. Intermittent fasting. Every system promised the same thing: if you just follow the rules, you'll finally be in control. And every system failed, because they were all trying to silence the part instead of listening to her.

Wild, right? The more I tried to shut her up, the louder she got. The more I restricted, the more she binged. The more I judged her, the more she needed food to soothe the shame I was piling on top of the original pain.

Dan Siegel, a clinical professor of psychiatry at UCLA, talks about "mindsight" - the ability to see the mind itself. He says that when we can name an emotion, we can regulate it. But when we can't name it, the emotion stays in the body as a vague, overwhelming pressure. That pressure demands release. And if we don't have words, we use actions. Food. Alcohol. Scrolling. Shopping. Anything to discharge the energy.

So the work isn't about food. The work is about language. It's about sitting with that part and asking her, genuinely, with curiosity instead of contempt: "What are you feeling? What are you trying to protect me from? What happened right before you reached for that bag of chips?"

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). Jonice Webb wrote it to help people who grew up in homes where their emotional needs were never acknowledged. If you don't know what you're feeling because no one ever taught you, how can you possibly regulate without food? You can't. Not until you learn.

How to be with the part instead of against her

I'm not going to tell you to stop using food to cope. That would be like telling someone with a broken leg to stop using crutches. The crutches aren't the problem. The broken bone is. And until the bone heals, you need the crutches.

But you can start building a different relationship with that part. Here's what that looks like for me:

When I notice myself reaching for food without hunger, I pause. Not to stop myself. Just to notice. I put my hand on my chest. I take one breath. And I say, out loud or in my head: "I see you. I know you're trying to help. What's happening right now?"

Sometimes the answer is obvious. I'm exhausted. I'm lonely. I'm scared about something I can't control. Sometimes the answer is a vague sensation in my body, a pressure in my throat, a tightness in my shoulders. I don't need to name it perfectly. I just need to be with it.

Then I ask the part what she needs. Not what she wants. What she needs. Food is what she wants. But what she needs is usually something else. Safety. Connection. Rest. Someone to witness her pain. Someone to say "that was hard and you didn't deserve it."

I can't always give her those things. But I can acknowledge that she deserves them. And that acknowledgment, over time, starts to build trust. She starts to believe that maybe, just maybe, I'm not going to abandon her the way everyone else did.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did something soften? Did something tighten? That's the part responding. She's listening. She's watching to see if you're going to reject her again or if you're finally going to see her.

There is no finish line

I've been doing this work for years. I still have nights where the refrigerator light is the only comfort I can find. I still have moments where I eat past fullness, chasing a feeling I can't name. The difference is that I don't hate myself for it anymore. I don't wake up the next morning and swear I'll never do it again. I wake up and I say, "That was hard. What was hard about yesterday?"

Sit with that. The goal is not to stop using food to manage emotions. The goal is to slowly, gently, expand your capacity to be with those emotions without needing food as a firefighter. And that takes time. It takes practice. It takes failing over and over and over again.

But every time you pause instead of judging, every time you ask instead of attacking, you're building a new neural pathway. You're teaching your nervous system that there's another way. You're telling that part of you that she's not alone anymore.

And that, honestly, is the only thing that actually changes anything.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - A book that many find validates their childhood experience
  • Running on Empty (paid link) - For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
